topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT752 - FLUID POWER SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simple Circuits, Hydraulic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — SIMPLE HYDRAULIC CIRCUITS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Simple Hydraulic Circuit** is a closed, self-contained fluid power system that uses an incompressible working fluid (typically mineral-based hydraulic oil conforming to **ISO VG 32 / VG 46** viscosity grades) to transmit, control, and convert energy between a prime mover (electric motor + pump) and one or more linear (hydraulic cylinder) or rotary (hydraulic motor) actuators. The circuit must comply with **ISO 1219-1:2012** (graphic symbols) and **ISO 4413** (general rules for hydraulic systems and components).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** *Module 4 emphasizes the design logic, component selection, and functional analysis of elementary hydraulic circuits — including single-acting, double-acting, regenerative, speed-control, and pressure-control circuits.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a hydraulic circuit like the **human circulatory system**:
- The **pump** is the heart, pushing fluid continuously.
- The **oil reservoir** is the body's blood pool.
- The **valves** are the valves in veins, directing flow.
- The **cylinder** is a muscle, doing the actual mechanical work.
- The **relief valve** is a safety pressure release, like a pressure-release valve in the brain.

When you press a lever (directional control valve), fluid flows into a chamber, and a piston pushes outward — like squeezing a water-filled syringe to push a rod forward.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value/Range |
|---|---|
| **Hydraulic Oil Density** | **ρ ≈ 870 kg/m³** (at 40 °C) |
| **Bulk Modulus (Oil)** | **β ≈ 1.4 – 2.0 GPa** |
| **Kinematic Viscosity** | **ν = 32–46 cSt** (ISO VG 32/46) |
| **Standard Working Pressure** | **70 – 210 bar** (typical industrial) |
| **Standard Test Pressure** | **1.5 × Working Pressure** |
| **System Efficiency Target** | **η_total ≥ 80%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Hydraulic Circuit:** A combination of interconnected hydraulic components arranged to perform useful work, controlled by the flow rate (Q) and pressure (P) of the working fluid according to the fundamental power equation **P = ΔP × Q**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Building Blocks of a Simple Hydraulic Circuit

Every simple hydraulic circuit, regardless of complexity, contains the following five functional subsystems:

1. **Power Source (P):** Electric motor + hydraulic pump (fixed or variable displacement).
2. **Reservoir (T):** Storage, cooling, de-aeration, and contamination control of the fluid.
3. **Control Elements:** Directional Control Valves (DCV), Pressure Control Valves (PCV), Flow Control Valves (FCV).
4. **Actuator (A/B):** Single-acting cylinder (SAC), Double-acting cylinder (DAC), or hydraulic motor.
5. **Auxiliary Elements:** Filters, accumulators, pressure gauges, hoses, fittings, and heat exchangers.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Flow

- **Step 1:** The electric motor drives the pump, which draws oil from the reservoir through a suction strainer.
- **Step 2:** The pressurized oil is delivered to a Pressure Relief Valve (PRV), set slightly above maximum working pressure.
- **Step 3:** A Directional Control Valve (DCV) directs the flow either to the cap-end (head end) or rod-end of the cylinder.
- **Step 4:** The cylinder piston converts fluid pressure into linear mechanical force.
- **Step 5:** Exhaust oil returns to the reservoir through a return-line filter.
- **Step 6:** A Flow Control Valve (FCV) regulates actuator speed by metering flow rate.

### 2.3 Classification of Simple Hydraulic Circuits

| Circuit Type | Primary Function | Key Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Acting Cylinder Circuit** | Extension via pump; retraction by gravity/spring | 3/2 DCV, check valve |
| **Double-Acting Cylinder Circuit** | Powered extension & retraction | 4/2 or 4/3 DCV |
| **Regenerative Circuit** | Faster extension using both pump & exhaust flow | 4/3 DCV (tandem center) + check valves |
| **Speed Control Circuit** | Meter-in / Meter-out speed regulation | FCV, DCV, check valve |
| **Pressure Control Circuit** | Pressure sequencing, unloading, counterbalancing | PCV (relief, sequence, unloading, counterbalance) |
| **Synchronizing Circuit** | Equal motion of two cylinders | Flow dividers, equal-area cylinders |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = P \times A$ | Force = Pressure × Piston Area | N, Pa, m² | Cylinder thrust force |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times D^{2}$ | Full bore (cap-end) area | m² | Effective piston area |
| 3 | $A_{r} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (D^{2} - d^{2})$ | Annular (rod-end) area | m² | Effective rod-side area |
| 4 | $Q = A \times v$ | Flow = Area × Velocity | m³/s | Cylinder extension speed |
| 5 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Velocity of piston | m/s | Speed calculation |
| 6 | $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \times Q$ | Hydraulic Power | W | Power transmission |
| 7 | $t = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{A \times L}{Q}$ | Cylinder stroke time | s | Time delay |
| 8 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | % | Pump/cylinder losses |
| 9 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{theoretical}}$ | Mechanical efficiency | % | Friction losses |
| 10 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech}$ | Overall efficiency | % | System performance |
| 11 | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{\eta_{pump}}{Q_{pump} \times p_{pump}}$ | Pump input power | W | Motor sizing |
| 12 | $\Delta P_{line} = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi d^{4}}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille line loss | Pa | Pressure drop in pipe |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU 2024 Note:** When using formula 1 in problems, always use **gauge pressure** (not absolute), and account for the **back-pressure** in the return line when computing rod-end force.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Injection Molding Machines:** Clamping and injection units use double-acting cylinders with regenerative circuits.
- **Earth-moving Equipment (JCB, Excavators):** Multiple DAC circuits with counterbalance valves for safe load holding.
- **Aircraft Landing Gear:** Hydraulic DAC systems with sequence valves for synchronized retraction.
- **Hydraulic Presses:** High-pressure circuits (up to **700 bar**) with pressure intensification.
- **Ship Steering Gear:** Double-acting cylinders with follow-up control.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cylinder Output Force

The most fundamental relationship in hydraulic circuits. Pressure acting on a piston area produces linear force.

**Given:**
- Cylinder bore diameter: $D$ (m)
- System pressure: $P$ (Pa)
- Rod diameter: $d$ (m)

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify the piston face area exposed to pressure.} \\
\text{Cap-end (full bore) area} \quad A_{c} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^{2} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Apply Pascal's Law — pressure acts equally on all areas.} \\
F_{c} &= P \times A_{c} \\[6pt]
F_{c} &= P \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^{2} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: For rod-end, subtract the rod cross-section.} \\
A_{r} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (D^{2} - d^{2}) \\[6pt]
F_{r} &= P \times A_{r} = P \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (D^{2} - d^{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The rod-end force is always less than the cap-end force for the **same pressure** because the effective area is reduced by the rod.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cylinder Extension/Retraction Velocity

Flow rate entering the cylinder chamber determines piston velocity.

**Given:** Flow rate $Q$ (m³/s), Cylinder bore $D$ (m), Rod diameter $d$ (m).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Volumetric flow into a chamber = Area × Velocity.} \\
Q &= A \times v \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Solve for velocity during extension (cap-end fill).} \\
v_{ext} &= \frac{Q}{A_{c}} = \frac{Q}{\frac{\pi}{4} \times D^{2}} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Solve for velocity during retraction (rod-end fill).} \\
v_{ret} &= \frac{Q}{A_{r}} = \frac{4Q}{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stroke Time Calculation

**Given:** Cylinder bore $D$ (m), Rod diameter $d$ (m), Stroke $L$ (m), Pump flow $Q$ (m³/s).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Volume of oil required to fill cap-end chamber.} \\
V_{c} &= A_{c} \times L = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^{2} \times L \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Time for full extension (assuming no losses).} \\
t_{ext} &= \frac{V_{c}}{Q} = \frac{\pi D^{2} L}{4Q} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Time for full retraction.} \\
t_{ret} &= \frac{V_{r}}{Q} = \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2}) L}{4Q}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Since $A_{r} < A_{c}$, it follows that $t_{ret} < t_{ext}$ for the same flow rate. Therefore, **retraction is always faster** than extension (for the same Q).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hydraulic Power & Pump Sizing

**Given:** System pressure $P$ (Pa), Flow rate $Q$ (m³/s), Pump efficiency $\eta_{pump}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Hydraulic (output) power of the pump.} \\
P_{hyd} &= \Delta P \times Q \quad [\text{Watts}] \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Mechanical (input) power required at pump shaft.} \\
P_{mech} &= \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{\Delta P \times Q}{\eta_{pump}} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Electric motor power rating (with motor efficiency).} \\
P_{motor} &= \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump} \times \eta_{motor}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 80$ mm, rod diameter $d = 45$ mm, stroke $L = 500$ mm. The pump delivers $Q = 40$ L/min at a system pressure of $P = 120$ bar. Calculate: (a) Extension force, (b) Retraction force, (c) Extension time, (d) Retraction time, (e) Hydraulic power.

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 0.080 \text{ m}, \quad d = 0.045 \text{ m}, \quad L = 0.500 \text{ m} \\
P &= 120 \text{ bar} = 120 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 1.20 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \\
Q &= 40 \text{ L/min} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(a) Extension Force (Cap-end):} \\
A_{c} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.080)^{2} \\
&= 0.7854 \times 0.0064 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} \\[6pt]
F_{c} &= P \times A_{c} = 1.20 \times 10^{7} \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 60{,}319 \text{ N} \approx 60.32 \text{ kN} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(b) Retraction Force (Rod-end):} \\
A_{r} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (D^{2} - d^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.0064 - 0.002025) \\
&= 0.7854 \times 0.004375 = 3.437 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} \\[6pt]
F_{r} &= P \times A_{r} = 1.20 \times 10^{7} \times 3.437 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 41{,}240 \text{ N} \approx 41.24 \text{ kN} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(c) Extension Time:} \\
t_{ext} &= \frac{\pi D^{2} L}{4Q} = \frac{\pi \times (0.080)^{2} \times 0.500}{4 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= \frac{0.01005}{2.667 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.77 \text{ s} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(d) Retraction Time:} \\
t_{ret} &= \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2}) L}{4Q} = \frac{\pi \times 0.004375 \times 0.500}{4 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= \frac{0.006873}{2.667 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.58 \text{ s} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(e) Hydraulic Power:} \\
P_{hyd} &= \Delta P \times Q = 1.20 \times 10^{7} \times 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 8000 \text{ W} = 8.00 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Results Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Extension Force $F_{c}$ | **60.32 kN** |
| Retraction Force $F_{r}$ | **41.24 kN** |
| Extension Time $t_{ext}$ | **3.77 s** |
| Retraction Time $t_{ret}$ | **2.58 s** |
| Hydraulic Power $P_{hyd}$ | **8.00 kW** |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Simple Circuit Types

| Circuit | Valve Used | Pump Type | Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single-Acting | 3/2 DCV | Fixed | Simple, cheap | Lower force, gravity dependent |
| Double-Acting | 4/2 DCV | Fixed | Force both ways | More components |
| Regenerative | 4/3 DCV (Tandem) | Fixed | Faster extension | Reduced extension force |
| Meter-in Speed | 2-way FCV | Fixed | Controlled speed | Heat generation |
| Meter-out Speed | 2-way FCV | Fixed | Prevents over-running | Cavitation risk |
| Counterbalance | Pilot-operated PCV | Fixed | Holds load safely | Heat dissipation |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Simple Hydraulic Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[M1: Electric Motor 3-phase 415V] --> B[P1: Hydraulic Pump Fixed/Variable]
    B --> C[T1: Reservoir 200L capacity]
    C --> B
    B --> D[V1: Relief Valve Set 150 bar]
    D --> E[V2: DCV 4/3 Solenoid]
    E --> F[F1: Flow Control Valve Meter-in]
    F --> G[A1: Double-Acting Cylinder Bore 80mm]
    G --> E
    E --> H[F2: Return Line Filter 10 micron]
    H --> C
    G --> I[Load External 50kN]
    E --> J[V3: Pressure Gauge 0-250 bar]
    E --> K[V4: Accumulator 10L pre-charge 100 bar]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Reading:** Follow the arrows in the direction of **power flow** (pump → control → actuator → return). This is the KTU-recommended way to sketch functional schematics in theory exams.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Single-Acting Cylinder Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1([P: Pump]) --> PRV{V1: Relief Valve}
    PRV --> DCV1{V2: 3-2 DCV Spring Offset}
    DCV1 -->|Energized| SAC1[A: Single-Acting Cylinder]
    DCV1 -->|De-energized| RET1[Return to Tank]
    SAC1 -->|Spring Return| RET1
    PRV --> RET1
    RET1 --> T1([T: Reservoir])
    SAC1 --> LOAD1((Load Lifts Up))
```

**Operating Sequence:**

| Step | DCV Position | Cylinder Action | Fluid Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal (Spring Offset) | Up | Pump → Tank (unloaded) |
| 2 | Actuated | Down | Pump → Cap-end → Cylinder extends |
| 3 | Released | Up (Spring) | Cylinder retracts → Tank |

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Double-Acting Cylinder Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PUMP([P1: Pump]) --> RV{V1: Relief Valve}
    RV --> DCV{V2: 4-3 DCV Closed Center}
    DCV -->|Position A| CAP[A1 Cap-end Line]
    DCV -->|Position B| ROD[A2 Rod-end Line]
    CAP --> CYL[Cylinder Bore D 80mm]
    ROD --> CYL
    CYL --> LOAD((External Load 60kN))
    DCV --> FCV{V3: FCV Meter-out}
    FCV --> FILTER[F1: Return Filter 10 micron]
    FILTER --> TANK([T1: Reservoir 200L])
    RV --> TANK
    DCV --> PG((PG: Pressure Gauge))
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Regenerative Circuit (Speed Boost)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PUMP([P1: Pump Q 40 L/min]) --> DCV{V2: 4-3 DCV Tandem Center}
    DCV -->|Path 1| CV1{CV1: Check Valve}
    CV1 --> CAP[A1: Cap-end]
    CAP --> CYL[Cylinder Piston]
    CYL --> ROD[A2: Rod-end]
    ROD --> CV2{CV2: Check Valve}
    ROD --> DCV
    CV1 --> DCV
    DCV --> RET[Return Line]
    RET --> TANK([T1: Reservoir])
```

**How Regeneration Works (KTU 2024 Concept):**

| Phase | Flow Path | Effective Q | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Without Regen | Pump → Cap-end only | $Q$ | Normal speed |
| With Regen | Pump + Rod-end → Cap-end | $Q + Q_{rod}$ | **2× speed (approx.)** |
| Trade-off | Force reduced by ratio $A_{r}/A_{c}$ | — | Lower thrust |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Topic:** Regenerative circuits are commonly asked in Part B. Remember the force reduction factor equals $A_{r}/A_{c}$.

---

## 4.5 Circuit Symbols Reference (ISO 1219-1)

| Component | ISO Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Pump (Fixed) | Circle with solid arrow | Constant displacement |
| Pump (Variable) | Circle with diagonal arrow | Variable displacement |
| DCV 4/3 | Two adjacent squares with arrows | Directional control |
| Relief Valve | Square with diagonal arrow + spring | Pressure limiting |
| Check Valve | Ball-in-cone symbol | One-way flow |
| Flow Control | Throttle symbol (bow-tie) | Speed regulation |
| Accumulator | Circle with spring + piston | Energy storage |
| Filter | Dashed rectangle with X | Contamination control |
| Cylinder (DAC) | Rectangle with piston rod | Linear actuator |
| Reservoir | Open trapezoid | Fluid storage |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define a hydraulic circuit. List any four essential components of a simple hydraulic circuit with one-line functions.**

**Model Answer:**

A hydraulic circuit is a closed-loop arrangement of hydraulic components that transmits power using pressurized fluid according to $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \times Q$.

**Essential Components:**

1. **Hydraulic Pump** — Converts mechanical energy to hydraulic energy.
2. **Reservoir** — Stores, cools, and de-aerates the fluid.
3. **Directional Control Valve (DCV)** — Directs fluid flow path.
4. **Actuator (Cylinder/Motor)** — Converts hydraulic energy back to mechanical work.

*(Acceptable: Relief Valve, Filter, Accumulator, Flow Control Valve — any four valid)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between meter-in and meter-out speed control circuits in hydraulics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Meter-In Control | Meter-Out Control |
|---|---|---|
| Location of FCV | On inlet line to actuator | On outlet line from actuator |
| Controls | Flow entering actuator | Flow leaving actuator |
| Best For | Resistance loads | Over-running loads |
| Cavitation Risk | Low | Higher (if mis-tuned) |
| Heat Dissipation | Heat enters actuator | Heat stays in return line |
| Typical Use | Horizontal pushing | Vertical lowering with load |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]

**Q: (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **double-acting cylinder circuit** using a 4/3 directional control valve. List the function of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 100$ mm and rod diameter $d = 60$ mm. It operates at a pressure of 140 bar with a pump flow of 60 L/min. Calculate the extension force, retraction force, extension time, and retraction time for a stroke of 600 mm. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a) — Double-Acting Cylinder Circuit

**Components and Functions:**

1. **Pump (P1):** Delivers pressurized oil (Q = 60 L/min, P = 140 bar).
2. **Reservoir (T1):** Stores hydraulic oil ISO VG 46; capacity 250 L.
3. **Relief Valve (V1):** Safety device set at 150 bar to protect against overpressure.
4. **4/3 DCV (V2):** Directs flow to either cap-end (extend) or rod-end (retract). Spring-centred, solenoid-operated.
5. **Double-Acting Cylinder (A1):** Bore 100 mm, Stroke 600 mm. Performs linear work.
6. **Pressure Gauge (PG):** Monitors system pressure (0–250 bar).
7. **Return Filter (F1):** 10-micron filtration, ISO 4406 18/16/13 cleanliness.

**Working (Extension Phase — DCV in Position A):**

- Solenoid A energised → DCV shifts to left position.
- Pump flow enters cap-end of cylinder.
- Piston extends; rod-end oil returns to tank through DCV.

**Working (Retraction Phase — DCV in Position B):**

- Solenoid B energised → DCV shifts to right position.
- Pump flow enters rod-end of cylinder.
- Piston retracts; cap-end oil returns to tank through DCV.

**Neutral (Spring-Centred):** Pump flow unloaded to tank; cylinder is hydraulically locked.

---

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical Calculations

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 0.100 \text{ m}, \quad d = 0.060 \text{ m}, \quad L = 0.600 \text{ m} \\
P &= 140 \text{ bar} = 1.40 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \\
Q &= 60 \text{ L/min} = 1.00 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calculations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(i) Extension Force (Cap-end):} \\
A_{c} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.100)^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} \\
F_{ext} &= P \times A_{c} = 1.40 \times 10^{7} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 1.0996 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} = \mathbf{109.96 \text{ kN}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(ii) Retraction Force (Rod-end):} \\
A_{r} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.100^{2} - 0.060^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 \\
&= 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} \\
F_{ret} &= 1.40 \times 10^{7} \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 7.037 \times 10^{4} \text{ N} = \mathbf{70.37 \text{ kN}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(iii) Extension Time:} \\
t_{ext} &= \frac{\pi D^{2} L}{4Q} = \frac{\pi \times 0.01 \times 0.600}{4 \times 1.00 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{0.01885}{4.00 \times 10^{-3}} = \mathbf{4.71 \text{ s}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(iv) Retraction Time:} \\
t_{ret} &= \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2}) L}{4Q} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064 \times 0.600}{4 \times 1.00 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{0.01206}{4.00 \times 10^{-3}} = \mathbf{3.02 \text{ s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Extension Force | **109.96 kN** |
| Retraction Force | **70.37 kN** |
| Extension Time | **4.71 s** |
| Retraction Time | **3.02 s** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam – July 2023 Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Explain the principle, construction, and working of a **regenerative circuit** with a neat circuit diagram. State two advantages and one disadvantage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic press uses a single-acting cylinder of bore 150 mm. The pump delivers 25 L/min at 200 bar. Calculate: (i) Maximum downward thrust, (ii) Maximum extension velocity, (iii) Stroke time for 300 mm travel, (iv) Hydraulic power. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a) — Regenerative Circuit

**Principle:** A regenerative circuit reuses the oil exhausting from the rod-end of the cylinder by routing it back to the cap-end (combined with pump flow), effectively doubling the extension flow rate and increasing speed.

**Construction (using ISO 1219 symbols):**
- Fixed displacement pump.
- 4/3 DCV with **tandem-center** position.
- Two check valves (CV1 and CV2).
- Double-acting cylinder.
- Pressure relief valve for safety.

**Working (Extension — Regenerative Mode):**
- DCV in regenerative position connects rod-end return to cap-end inlet.
- Pump flow $Q$ + rod-end flow $Q_{r}$ both enter cap-end.
- Effective flow into cap-end = $Q_{total} = Q + Q \times (A_{r}/A_{c})$.
- **Result:** Extension speed increases by factor of $(1 + A_{r}/A_{c})$.

**Working (Retraction — Normal Mode):**
- DCV shifts; pump flow goes to rod-end.
- Cap-end oil returns to tank through DCV.

**Advantages:**
1. **Faster extension** without using a larger pump (energy-efficient).
2. **Smaller pump capacity** required for the same speed.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Reduced extension force** because the rod-end back-pressure opposes the cap-end pressure. Effective force = $P \times A_{r}$ (instead of $P \times A_{c}$).

---

#### Solution to (b) — Hydraulic Press Numerical

**Given:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 0.150 \text{ m}, \quad L = 0.300 \text{ m} \\
P &= 200 \text{ bar} = 2.00 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \\
Q &= 25 \text{ L/min} = 4.167 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calculations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(i) Maximum Downward Thrust:} \\
A &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.150)^{2} = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^{2} \\
F &= P \times A = 2.00 \times 10^{7} \times 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \\
&= 3.534 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} = \mathbf{353.4 \text{ kN}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(ii) Maximum Extension Velocity:} \\
v &= \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}{1.767 \times 10^{-2}} \\
&= 2.358 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/s} = \mathbf{23.58 \text{ mm/s}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(iii) Stroke Time:} \\
t &= \frac{A \times L}{Q} = \frac{1.767 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.300}{4.167 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= \frac{5.301 \times 10^{-3}}{4.167 \times 10^{-4}} = \mathbf{12.72 \text{ s}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(iv) Hydraulic Power:} \\
P_{hyd} &= \Delta P \times Q = 2.00 \times 10^{7} \times 4.167 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 8333 \text{ W} = \mathbf{8.33 \text{ kW}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error (Major):** Students frequently write $P = 140$ bar directly in the formula $F = P \times A$ without converting to Pa. This leads to a 10⁵ magnitude error and **3-mark deduction** in numericals.
> 2. **Mixing up Rod-end and Cap-end areas:** Always remember $A_{r} < A_{c}$. The rod-end area uses $(D^{2} - d^{2})$, not $d^{2}$ alone.
> 3. **Forgetting volumetric efficiency:** In real KTU problems with given $\eta_{vol}$, divide theoretical flow by efficiency before computing velocity: $Q_{actual} = Q_{pump} \times \eta_{vol}$.
> 4. **Missing return-line back-pressure:** When the question specifies a "back-pressure of X bar" in the tank line, subtract it from rod-end calculations.
> 5. **Sketch missing components:** A circuit drawn without **relief valve, filter, and reservoir labeling** loses 1–2 marks in 7-mark descriptive questions.
> 6. **Confusing regenerative force with normal force:** In regen circuits, force is **reduced** to $F = P \times A_{r}$, not $F = P \times A_{c}$. This is a very common 2-mark error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Pascal's Law** is the foundation: pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted equally in all directions.
- ✅ The **fundamental power equation** is $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \times Q$ (W), where $\Delta P$ is in Pa and $Q$ in m³/s.
- ✅ **Cap-end area** $A_{c} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^{2}$ ; **Rod-end area** $A_{r} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^{2} - d^{2})$.
- ✅ Cylinder **force**: $F = P \times A$ ; **velocity**: $v = Q/A$ ; **time**: $t = A \times L / Q$.
- ✅ **Retraction is always faster** than extension at the same flow rate (smaller area to fill).
- ✅ **Retraction force is always less** than extension force at the same pressure.
- ✅ **Regenerative circuit** = faster extension + reduced force (force = $P \times A_{r}$).
- ✅ **Meter-in** controls speed by throttling inlet flow; **Meter-out** controls outlet flow.
- ✅ **Relief valve** is a safety device — must always be present in a hydraulic circuit.
- ✅ **Filter placement**: suction strainer (100 μm) + pressure filter (10 μm) + return filter (25 μm) is the KTU-recommended three-stage filtration.
- ✅ **ISO 1219** governs symbols; **ISO 4413** governs hydraulic safety rules.
- ✅ Standard cleanliness target: **ISO 4406 — 18/16/13** (NAS 7 approx.).
- ✅ Always convert bar → Pa: multiply by $10^{5}$.
- ✅ Always convert L/min → m³/s: multiply by $10^{-3}/60 = 1.667 \times 10^{-5}$.
- ✅ **Pump volumetric efficiency** $\eta_{vol} = Q_{actual}/Q_{theoretical}$ (typically 0.92–0.98).
- ✅ **Counterbalance valve** = back-pressure holding valve for vertical loads.
- ✅ **Sequence valve** = pressure-operated valve that opens at a preset pressure.
- ✅ **Unloading valve** = diverts pump flow to tank at low pressure, saving energy.

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — SIMPLE HYDRAULIC CIRCUITS**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 guidelines, and ISO 4413 safety standards.*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT752 - FLUID POWER SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pneumatic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pneumatic System:** A pneumatic system is a branch of **fluid power** that uses a **compressible fluid** — specifically **compressed atmospheric air** — as the working medium to transmit, control, and convert energy into mechanical work through pressure differentials. The term originates from the Greek word *pneuma*, meaning "breath" or "wind."

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT752 – Fluid Power Safety)**, pneumatics is formally defined as the engineering discipline dealing with the generation, transmission, conditioning, and control of **compressed air** (typically at working pressures between **4 bar to 8 bar**, with the KTU-recommended standard being **6 bar**) to perform linear or rotary mechanical work through **pneumatic actuators** (cylinders and motors), while ensuring adherence to **safety protocols** prescribed under **IS 13056:1991** and **ISO 4414:2010**.

The governing physical principle is **Pascal's Law** (transmission of pressure equally in all directions) applied to a **compressible medium**, which distinguishes pneumatics fundamentally from hydraulics (which uses incompressible fluids).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a pneumatic system as the "lungs and muscles" of modern industry.** Just as human breathing uses compressed air (your lungs) to push muscles (actuators) to move objects, an industrial pneumatic system uses an air compressor (lungs) to push cylinders (muscles) to clamp, lift, push, or rotate workpieces.

### The "Bicycle Pump" Analogy

Imagine you have a **bicycle pump** connected to a sealed cylinder with a piston:

| Real-World Object | Pneumatic Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Your body pushing the pump handle | **Electric Motor / Engine** | Prime mover supplying mechanical energy |
| The pump barrel | **Air Compressor** | Converts mechanical energy → pneumatic (pressure) energy |
| Air inside the tube | **Compressed Air (Working Fluid)** | Stores and transmits energy |
| The bicycle tire valve | **Direction Control Valve (DCV)** | Allows/restricts flow direction |
| The tire inflating | **Pneumatic Cylinder** | Converts pneumatic energy → mechanical work |
| Pressure gauge on tire | **Pressure Gauge / Sensor** | Monitors system state |

> **Just as a tire becomes "hard" because air molecules are forced closer together, a pneumatic system becomes "powerful" because the compressor forces air molecules into a smaller volume, creating pressure that can do work.**

---

## 1.3 Why Compressed Air? — The Engineering Rationale

> [!NOTE]
> Compressed air is the **4th utility** in modern industry, after electricity, water, and gas, because it is **abundant (78% of atmosphere is nitrogen, 21% oxygen)**, **safely transportable** (no fire hazard unlike oil), and **temperature-tolerant** (works from $-30^\circ C$ to $+200^\circ C$).

### Key Physical Properties of Air as a Working Fluid

| Property | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Density at STP | **$\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$** | Very low → low inertia → fast response |
| Viscosity (dynamic) | **$\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$** | Negligible friction losses in pipes |
| Compressibility | **High (Isentropic exponent $\gamma = 1.4$)** | Allows energy storage in tanks |
| Specific Gas Constant | **$R = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$** | Used in ideal gas law $PV = mRT$ |
| Standard Working Pressure | **$P_{std} = 6 \text{ bar (600 kPa)}$** | KTU/Industry standard reference |
| Maximum Practical Pressure | **$P_{max} = 10 \text{ bar}$** | Above this → safety hazard |

---

## 1.4 Pneumatics vs. Hydraulics — A Comparative Snapshot

| Parameter | Pneumatic System | Hydraulic System |
|---|---|---|
| Working Fluid | **Air (compressible)** | **Oil (incompressible)** |
| Operating Pressure | **Low (4–8 bar)** | **High (70–350 bar)** |
| Force Output | **Low (up to ~25 kN)** | **Very High (up to ~500 kN)** |
| Speed | **Very High (1–2 m/s)** | **Moderate (0.5 m/s)** |
| Precision | **Moderate** | **Very High** |
| Safety | **High (no fire risk)** | **Moderate (oil leak/fire risk)** |
| Cost | **Low** | **High** |
| Return Line | **Not required (exhaust to atmosphere)** | **Required (return tank)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Safety Focus:** Pneumatic systems are inherently safer than hydraulic systems for low-to-medium force applications because (a) air leaks do not create fire/slip hazards, (b) there is no return line, and (c) pressure drops rapidly upon compressor shutdown.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Pneumatic System

A complete pneumatic system, as required by **KTU 2024 Module 4**, consists of **four functional blocks**. Every component fits into one of these categories:

### Block 1: **Power Generation Unit (PGU)**

The **air compressor** is the heart of the pneumatic system. Its function is to convert mechanical (rotary) energy into pneumatic (pressure) energy by reducing the volume of atmospheric air.

**Types of Compressors (KTU-relevant):**

| Type | Working Principle | Pressure Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reciprocating (Piston)** | Piston compresses air in cylinder | Up to 10 bar | Workshops, small industry |
| **Rotary (Vane/Screw)** | Rotating element traps and compresses air | Up to 13 bar | Continuous duty plants |
| **Centrifugal** | Impeller imparts kinetic energy | Above 10 bar | Large-scale plants |

### Block 2: **Air Treatment Unit (ATU) — The "FRL"**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FRL = Filter + Regulator + Lubricator.** This is the **mandatory safety-critical subassembly** that conditions compressed air before it enters the control valves. The KTU syllabus explicitly tests the function of each element.

| Component | Symbol | Function | Failure Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Filter** | Trapezoid with screen | Removes moisture, dust, contaminants | Valve jamming, actuator wear |
| **Regulator** | Spring-loaded diaphragm | Maintains constant downstream pressure ($P_{out} = P_{set}$) | Uncontrolled actuator force |
| **Lubricator** | Wick/baffle type | Atomizes oil into air stream | Piston seal failure |

> The combined unit is called an **FRL Assembly** and must be installed **immediately downstream** of the receiver tank.

### Block 3: **Control Elements (Valves)**

Valves regulate the **direction**, **pressure**, and **flow** of compressed air. They are classified using the **ISO 1219:2012** standard.

**A. Direction Control Valves (DCV)** — Symbolized by **N/2 or N/3** notation:
- **N** = Number of ports (2, 3, 4, or 5)
- **Number** = Number of positions (2 or 3)

> Example: A **5/2 DCV** has **5 ports** and **2 positions**. It is the most common valve for controlling a **double-acting cylinder**.

| Port Label | Function |
|---|---|
| **1 (P)** | Pressure inlet (from FRL) |
| **2 (A)** | Outlet to cap end of cylinder |
| **4 (B)** | Outlet to rod end of cylinder |
| **3 (R)** | Exhaust from cap end |
| **5 (S)** | Exhaust from rod end |

**B. Pressure Control Valves:**
- **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV):** Limits maximum system pressure (set at **1.1 × working pressure**).
- **Pressure Regulator:** Adjusts downstream pressure.
- **Sequence Valve:** Opens only when a preset pressure is reached.

**C. Flow Control Valves:**
- **Meter-in:** Controls air entering cylinder (controls extension speed).
- **Meter-out:** Controls air leaving cylinder (controls retraction speed).

### Block 4: **Actuators (Output Devices)**

| Type | Motion | Force Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Acting Cylinder** | Spring-return, one air port | $F = P \times A_{piston} - F_{spring}$ |
| **Double-Acting Cylinder** | Two air ports, no spring | $F_{ext} = P \times A_{piston}$, $F_{ret} = P \times (A_{piston} - A_{rod})$ |
| **Rotary Actuator** | Limited rotation (≤ 360°) | $T = P \times A \times r$ |
| **Pneumatic Motor** | Continuous rotation | $P_{out} = T \times \omega$ |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the formulas below.** They are the **most frequently tested** derivations in KTU Module 4 (Pneumatic) university examinations.

| # | Formula | Variables | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = P \times A$ | Force, Pressure, Piston Area | N, Pa, m² | Cylinder thrust force |
| 2 | $A_{piston} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Piston diameter | m² | Area for double-acting extension |
| 3 | $A_{rod} = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ | Bore, Rod diameter | m² | Area for double-acting retraction |
| 4 | $V_{cylinder} = A \times L$ | Volume per stroke | m³ | Air consumption per cycle |
| 5 | $Q = A \times v$ | Flow rate, Area, Velocity | m³/s | Compressor sizing |
| 6 | $W = P \times V$ | Work done per stroke | J | Mechanical work output |
| 7 | $P_{power} = \dfrac{F \times v}{\eta}$ | Power delivered | W | Actuator power |
| 8 | $V_{air} = V_{cylinder} \times \dfrac{P_{abs,working}}{P_{abs,atm}}$ | Free air delivery | m³/min | Compressor FAD calculation |
| 9 | $\text{Force Ratio} = \dfrac{F_{ret}}{F_{ext}} = \dfrac{D^2 - d^2}{D^2} = 1 - (d/D)^2$ | Bore/rod ratio | — | Cylinder selection |
| 10 | $\eta_{volumetric} = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{theoretical}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | — | Compressor performance |
| 11 | $\text{Reynolds Number} = \rho v D / \mu$ | Flow regime indicator | dimensionless | Pipe flow classification |

> **Note on Units:** KTU follows **SI units** strictly. Always convert pressure from **bar → Pa** by multiplying by **$10^5$**. E.g., 6 bar = $6 \times 10^5$ Pa.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pneumatic systems are indispensable in:

- **Manufacturing Automation:** Clamping, pressing, riveting, stamping in automotive assembly lines.
- **Robotics:** End-effector actuation in pick-and-place robots.
- **Food & Pharma:** Clean, contamination-free actuation (no oil leakage risk).
- **Mining & Hazardous Areas:** Intrinsically safe (no spark risk) when air-driven.
- **Pneumatic Tools:** Jackhammers, impact wrenches, nail guns.
- **Process Control:** Diaphragm-actuated control valves in chemical plants.

# SECTION_3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cylinder Thrust Force During Extension

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Formulation):**
A double-acting pneumatic cylinder has a bore diameter $D = 100 \text{ mm}$ and a rod diameter $d = 40 \text{ mm}$. It operates at a gauge pressure of $P_g = 6 \text{ bar}$. Calculate:
1. The theoretical extension force.
2. The theoretical retraction force.
3. The force ratio (retraction/extension).

**Given Data:**
- $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $d = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$
- $P_g = 6 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $P_{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- Atmospheric pressure acts on the rod side during extension (retracting face).

---

### Step 1 — Compute the Piston Area (Cap End)

The full piston face is exposed to compressed air during extension:

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.01}{4}
$$

$$
A_p = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

---

### Step 2 — Compute the Effective Annular Area (Rod End)

During retraction, the effective area is reduced by the rod cross-section:

$$
A_r = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
$$

Substituting:

$$
A_r = \frac{\pi [(0.1)^2 - (0.04)^2]}{4} = \frac{\pi [0.01 - 0.0016]}{4}
$$

$$
A_r = \frac{\pi \times 0.0084}{4}
$$

$$
A_r = 6.597 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute the Extension Force

Using the fundamental pneumatic force equation $F = P \times A$:

$$
F_{ext} = P_g \times A_p
$$

$$
F_{ext} = (6 \times 10^5) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
F_{ext} = 4712.4 \text{ N} \approx 4.71 \text{ kN}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Compute the Retraction Force

During retraction, gauge pressure acts on the annular area:

$$
F_{ret} = P_g \times A_r
$$

$$
F_{ret} = (6 \times 10^5) \times (6.597 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
F_{ret} = 3958.2 \text{ N} \approx 3.96 \text{ kN}
$$

---

### Step 5 — Compute the Force Ratio

$$
\text{Force Ratio} = \frac{F_{ret}}{F_{ext}} = \frac{A_r}{A_p} = \frac{D^2 - d^2}{D^2}
$$

$$
\text{Force Ratio} = \frac{0.0084}{0.01} = 0.84
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The retraction force is **84% of the extension force**. This is a critical design consideration — if the load requires equal force in both directions, a cylinder with a **larger rod diameter** must be selected (or a different bore size chosen).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Air Consumption and Compressor Sizing

**Problem Statement:**
A factory operates **10 double-acting cylinders** of bore 100 mm, stroke 200 mm, at 6 bar gauge pressure. Each cylinder completes **20 cycles per minute**. Determine:
1. The free air delivery (FAD) required from the compressor.
2. The compressor power rating (assume overall efficiency $\eta = 0.7$).

**Given:**
- $N = 10$ cylinders
- $D = 0.1 \text{ m}$, $d = 0.04 \text{ m}$, $L = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- $P_g = 6 \text{ bar}$, $P_{atm} = 1 \text{ bar}$ (approx, for simplicity)
- Cycles per minute $= 20$
- $\eta = 0.7$

---

### Step 1 — Volume of Air per Stroke (Extension Side)

$$
V_{ext} = A_p \times L = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2 = 1.571 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 2 — Volume of Air per Stroke (Retraction Side)

$$
V_{ret} = A_r \times L = 6.597 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2 = 1.319 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 3 — Total Volume of Air per Cycle per Cylinder

$$
V_{cycle} = V_{ext} + V_{ret} = 1.571 \times 10^{-3} + 1.319 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
V_{cycle} = 2.890 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{cycle}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Convert to Free Air Delivery (at Atmospheric Pressure)

Using **Boyle's Law** ($P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$) — air at working pressure $P_{abs}$ expands to atmospheric $P_{atm}$:

$$
V_{free} = V_{cycle} \times \frac{P_{abs, working}}{P_{atm}} = V_{cycle} \times \frac{P_{atm} + P_g}{P_{atm}}
$$

$$
\frac{P_{abs, working}}{P_{atm}} = \frac{1 + 6}{1} = 7
$$

$$
V_{free} = 2.890 \times 10^{-3} \times 7 = 2.023 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{cycle}
$$

---

### Step 5 — Total Free Air Required for All Cylinders

With 10 cylinders and 20 cycles/min:

$$
\text{FAD} = N \times \text{Cycles/min} \times V_{free}
$$

$$
\text{FAD} = 10 \times 20 \times 2.023 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
\text{FAD} = 4.046 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}
$$

> **Engineering Recommendation:** Apply a **safety factor of 1.25** → Compressor must supply at least **5.06 m³/min**.

---

### Step 6 — Compressor Power Rating

For an adiabatic compression process:

$$
P_{compressor} = \frac{P_{atm} \times V_{free} \times \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right] \times n}{\eta}
$$

where $n = (\gamma-1)/\gamma = (1.4-1)/1.4 = 0.2857$.

Pressure ratio: $P_2/P_1 = 7$

$$
\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{0.2857} = 7^{0.2857} = 1.646
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_{comp} = \frac{(1 \times 10^5) \times 4.046 \times 10^{-2}/60 \times (1.646 - 1)}{0.7}
$$

Simplifying step by step:

$$
P_{comp} = \frac{(1 \times 10^5) \times (6.74 \times 10^{-4}) \times (0.646)}{0.7}
$$

$$
P_{comp} = \frac{43.55}{0.7} = 62.2 \text{ W (per m}^3/\text{min per cycle basis)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For complete derivation in KTU exams, the simpler isothermal form is often accepted: $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU Problem-Type Coverage

| Problem Type | Required Formulas | Key Steps | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cylinder force calculation | $F = PA$, $A = \pi D^2/4$ | Convert bar → Pa; use gauge vs. absolute | Forgetting rod area subtraction |
| Compressor sizing | $V_{free} = V_{cyl} \times P_{abs}/P_{atm}$ | Multiply by N, cycles/min, safety factor | Forgetting safety factor |
| Power calculation | $P = Fv/\eta$ | Use effective force and actual velocity | Using rated instead of actual speed |
| Valve selection | N/2 notation | Match ports to actuator type | Confusing 5/2 with 5/3 |
| Cycle time estimation | $t = L/v$ | Account for acceleration/deceleration | Assuming constant velocity |
| Pressure drop in pipes | Hagen-Poiseuille (laminar) or Darcy (turbulent) | Calculate Reynolds number first | Wrong friction factor |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Complete Pneumatic System

This Mermaid diagram maps the **interaction and signal/energy flow** between every component of an industrial pneumatic installation — from atmospheric air intake to the final mechanical work output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> A2[Air Filter Inlet]
    A2 --> B1[Compressor Unit]
    B1 --> B2[After Cooler]
    B2 --> B3[Receiver Tank with Safety Valve]
    B3 --> C1[Dryer Unit]
    C1 --> C2[FRL Filter]
    C2 --> C3[FRL Regulator]
    C3 --> C4[FRL Lubricator]
    C4 --> D1[Distribution Manifold]
    D1 --> E1[5 by 2 DCV Solenoid Operated]
    D1 --> E2[Pressure Relief Valve]
    D1 --> E3[Flow Control Valve Meter In]
    D1 --> E4[Flow Control Valve Meter Out]
    E1 --> F1[Double Acting Cylinder]
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Load Mechanical Work Output]
    E2 --> H1[Exhaust to Atmosphere Silenced]
    F1 --> H2[Exhaust to Atmosphere Silenced]
    B3 --> I1[Pressure Gauge]
    I1 --> J1[Control Panel PLC]
    J1 --> E1
```

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Direct Control of Double-Acting Cylinder (5/2 DCV)

This is the **most frequently drawn circuit** in KTU exams. It shows a manually-operated (push-button) 5/2 DCV controlling a double-acting cylinder with meter-in flow control on the extension stroke.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[1 Pressure Supply from FRL] --> V[5 by 2 Direction Control Valve]
    V -->|Port A| FC1[Flow Control Valve Meter In]
    FC1 --> CYL[Double Acting Cylinder]
    CYL -->|Port B| V
    V -->|Port R| EX1[Exhaust Silencer]
    V -->|Port S| EX2[Exhaust Silencer]
    SPR[Spring Return on DCV] -.-> V
```

---

## 4.3 Component-by-Component Symbol Legend (ISO 1219 Standard)

| Component | ISO Symbol Description | Mermaid Block |
|---|---|---|
| Compressor | Circle with rotating element | B1 |
| Receiver Tank | Cylindrical vessel with inlet/outlet | B3 |
| FRL Assembly | Stacked rectangles (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) | C2-C4 |
| 5/2 DCV | Two squares with arrows/triangle | E1 |
| PRV | Spring-loaded arrow against flow | E2 |
| Flow Control Valve | Throttle symbol (bow-tie) | E3, E4 |
| Double-Acting Cylinder | Rectangle with piston and rod | F1 |
| Silencer | Cone-shaped or hatched triangle | EX1, EX2 |
| Pressure Gauge | Circle with needle symbol | I1 |

---

## 4.4 Sequence-of-Operation Diagram (Cascading Cylinders)

A common KTU exam question asks to design a circuit where **Cylinder A extends, then Cylinder B extends, then both retract**. This is called a **cascade sequence** and is implemented using **pilot-operated limit valves** and a **5/2 sequence valve**.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Start as Start Push Button
    participant V1 as 5/2 DCV for Cyl A
    participant A as Cylinder A
    participant V2 as 5/2 DCV for Cyl B
    participant B as Cylinder B
    participant V3 as 5/2 DCV for Retract
    Start->>V1: Actuate
    V1->>A: Air to Port A
    A-->>V1: Limit valve 1A signal
    V1->>V2: Trigger B extension
    V2->>B: Air to Port A
    B-->>V2: Limit valve 1B signal
    V2->>V3: Trigger retract sequence
    V3->>A: Air to Port B
    V3->>B: Air to Port B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** When asked to "design a pneumatic circuit," always include **(1) supply line with FRL, (2) actuator, (3) control valves, (4) exhaust/silencer, and (5) limit switches if sequencing is required.**
